Overall:
The following processes were found to be somewhat effective for the removal of ESA, OXA, hydroxyacetochlor, deschloroacetochlor, 2-chloro-2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide, 2-hydroxy-2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide, and 2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide: granular activated carbon (27 to 52 percent) and ozone (55 to 73 percent). Powdered activated carbon was found to be somewhat effective in removing OXA and ESA (typically 20 to 80 percent). The use of chlorine and ozone were quite effective in removing 2-ethyl-6-methylaniline (100 percent).
Processes ineffective for the removal of ESA and OXA were chlorine (-76 to 16 percent), conventional treatment (-76 to 16 percent), and bank filtration (-26 to 30 percent). Chlorine (3 to 6 percent) and conventional treatment (4 to 6 percent) were also found to be ineffective in removing hydroxyacetochlor, deschloroacetochlor, 2-chloro-2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide, 2-hydroxy-2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide, and 2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide.
Biological treatment, which is not a typical drinking water treatment process, was suggestive of 2-ethyl-6-methylaniline removal.
